front: Philosopher: identifies the will-to-life with Kant's thing-in-itself as the fundamental reality underlying all phenomena, calling it 'blind incessant impulse'
Philosopher: illustrates the will's blind striving with the Australian bull-ant, which fights with itself after being cut in half
Philosopher: calls space and time the Scholastic 'principle of individuation' (principium individuationis) because multiplicity doesn't exist outside them
Philosopher: argues music is the highest art form because it directly copies the will, calling listening to it 'an unconscious exercise in metaphysics'
Philosopher: aesthetic contemplation releases one from the will; imagines viewing a tree or cliff transforms one into a 'timeless subject of knowledge'
Philosopher: the sublime bears a hostile relation to the will; identifies tragedy as the art form that draws out the 'highest degree' of the sublime
Philosopher: Richard Wagner changed the ending of the Ring Cycle after reading this thinker's book
Philosopher: rejected 11 of Kant's 12 categories as redundant, retaining only causality
Philosopher: argued Kant failed to distinguish abstract knowledge of concepts from direct perceptual knowledge of objects
Work: argues compassion (mitleid) is the only non-egoistic moral motive; was the only entry in a Royal Danish Society contest but rejected by an angry Hegelian
work: On the Basis of Morality (Compassion Ethics)

front: Philosopher: took the compatibilist position 'man can do what he wills but cannot will what he wills' in On the Freedom of the Will
work: On the Basis of Morality (Compassion Ethics)

front: Work: doctoral dissertation dividing the principle of sufficient reason into four roots: becoming, knowing, being, and willing
back: On the Fourfold Root of the Principle of Sufficient Reason (Schopenhauer)
work: On the Fourfold Root of the Principle of Sufficient Reason

front: Philosopher: used the example of porcupines huddling for warmth as a metaphor for the difficulties of human intimacy
work: Parerga and Paralipomena and Other Works

front: Philosopher: wrote 38 Ways to Win an Argument, a sarcastic debate guide including 'Become Personal, Insulting, and Rude'
work: Parerga and Paralipomena and Other Works

front: Philosopher: drew heavily on the Upanishads, repeatedly quoting 'tat tvam asi' ('this thou art') from the Chandogya Upanishad
Philosopher: Nietzsche praised this thinker 'as Educator' in the third of his Untimely Meditations
Philosopher: scheduled lectures at the same time as Hegel's out of spite but could never enroll enough students
Philosopher: owned a succession of poodles all named Atman/Atma, reflecting his interest in Indian thought
